easy.question: Who does the Leaseholder Assistance Policy apply to and what does it aim to provide?
easy.answer: It applies to Midland Heart leaseholders (excluding Retirement Living Plus and Mutuals) and to Right to Buy/Right to Acquire customers. It defines the advice, support or signposting Midland Heart may offer to leaseholders facing unforeseen financial, emotional or physiological difficulties. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

easy.question: What is the maximum length of a rent and service charge payment holiday (deferral) and what must be done first?
easy.answer: Up to 6 months, recoverable in subsequent payments. Before it can be considered, the leaseholder/shared owner must seek advice from Midland Heart’s Money Advice Service. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

easy.question: Is subletting allowed for Shared Owners?
easy.answer: The lease generally prohibits it, but Midland Heart may consent in exceptional circumstances (e.g., severe financial hardship or serious health impacts) after Money Advice input. Consent is time-limited, requires a letter of conditions, and a reputable agent; simple market slow-sales is not a reason. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

easy.question: Does Midland Heart routinely buy back properties or shares?
easy.answer: No—buy-backs or downward staircasing are only considered in exceptional, evidenced circumstances (e.g., severe hardship/health impacts) and after other options are exhausted. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

easy.question: List two common costs a leaseholder must pay if Midland Heart agrees a buy-back.
easy.answer: Legal fees and valuation fees (plus admin charge, six months’ rent and/or service charge from completion, and other reasonable fees such as stakeholder permission costs). :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

easy.question: What is ‘downward staircasing’?
easy.answer: Reducing the share owned by the shared owner (to a share above 0%); rent on Midland Heart’s share is reviewed/adjusted accordingly. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

easy.question: Who owns the policy and when is it next due for review?
easy.answer: Business owner: Head of Mutuals & Leasehold (Joel Pickersgill). Approved September 2024; next review due by 31 August 2027 (or sooner if required). :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

medium.question: A shared owner emails that a sudden illness has cut their income and they can’t cover both mortgage and rent/service charges for a few months. Outline the sequence of steps Midland Heart expects before granting any payment holiday.
medium.answer: They must first engage the Money Advice Service; evidence exceptional circumstances/severe financial hardship; Midland Heart weighs impacts (including on wider residents) and, if agreed, sets terms for a deferral up to 6 months to be recovered via later payments. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}

medium.question: Your lease prohibits subletting, but a shared owner requests short-term consent while they undergo treatment abroad. What conditions and checks apply?
medium.answer: Money Advice must be consulted; Midland Heart considers freeholder status and any limits under the Renters Reform Bill proposals; if consent is granted it’s not a long-term solution, requires a signed letter of conditions, use of a reputable agent, and the sub-tenant must comply with the lease. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

medium.question: A leaseholder seeks buy-back because their flat won’t sell. What baseline tests are applied before governance will even consider it?
medium.answer: They must evidence exceptional hardship/health impacts, show they cannot sell on the open market despite appropriate marketing, and have exhausted other routes; applications are not considered if they’re in arrears now or were within the last 12 months (unless arrears stem from the exceptional circumstances). :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}

medium.question: A block faces Major Works (Section 20). A few shared owners cannot front costs. What support might be available and what is the FCA limitation?
medium.answer: Midland Heart may consider proportionate assistance (e.g., signposting, evidence-based short-term arrangements), or downward staircasing for shared owners. It isn’t FCA-authorised to provide loans/interest-bearing options and won’t extend repayments beyond 12 months. :contentReference[oaicite:10]{index=10}

medium.question: Post buy-back, can the former leaseholder remain in the home?
medium.answer: Only subject to the Allocations Policy and any rent payable, because Midland Heart would then own the property outright. :contentReference[oaicite:11]{index=11}

medium.question: A RTB buyer asks if selling or subletting within five years has any financial consequence.
medium.answer: Yes—RTB/RTA discounts are repayable within five years if the property is sold or sublet. :contentReference[oaicite:12]{index=12}

hard.question: Design a proportionate response where: (a) a shared owner in severe ill-health requests (i) a 4-month payment holiday now, then (ii) temporary subletting for 6 months; and (b) if recovery fails, they want (iii) downward staircasing or (iv) buy-back.
hard.answer: (i) Require Money Advice engagement and hardship evidence; set a time-bound deferral (max 6 months) with clear recovery terms. (ii) Consider exceptional consent to sublet: check Midland Heart’s status as freeholder, policy limits, and government proposals; require a signed condition letter and reputable agent. (iii) If longer-term affordability fails, consider downward staircasing with adjusted rent on Midland Heart’s share. (iv) Buy-back only if exceptional criteria are met, arrears position is acceptable, all other avenues are exhausted, and governance approves; applicant pays listed fees. :contentReference[oaicite:13]{index=13}

hard.question: A leaseholder requests buy-back citing unaffordable Major Works and ill-health, but has had arrears twice in the last year unrelated to health. What is the likely decision path and timing, and what costs could still fall to them?
hard.answer: The recent arrears history blocks consideration unless they result from the exceptional circumstances. If accepted, governance approval is required (decision may take up to ~3 months plus 3–4 months to transact). If agreed, the leaseholder pays Midland Heart’s legal and valuation fees, admin charge, six months’ rent/service charge from completion and other reasonable fees (e.g., permissions). :contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}

hard.question: Explain how Midland Heart balances an individual’s request for assistance with its wider duties to other residents and organisational aims.
hard.answer: Decisions consider the leaseholder’s personal circumstances alongside impacts on wider residents (e.g., service charge flows), Midland Heart’s strategic aims (e.g., stock use), and current financial performance—applying discretion case-by-case. :contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}

hard.question: A shared owner delays Major Works citing hardship and asks Midland Heart to market their home. What does the policy allow and what alternative support exists?
hard.answer: Midland Heart will not act as an estate agent. It may signpost support, require evidence of hardship, consider short-term assistance within FCA limits, and for shared owners may consider downward staircasing to help fund works. :contentReference[oaicite:16]{index=16}

hard.question: Set out a governance-ready pack for an exceptional buy-back request so a decision can be made on first submission.
hard.answer: Include Money Advice assessment, evidence of severe hardship/health impacts, marketing history proving no open-market sale, arrears history with causation, confirmation of exhausted alternatives (incl. staircasing), legal/valuation estimates, service-charge implications for the block, and confirmation the applicant accepts all buy-back costs; note no appeal right after the governance decision. :contentReference[oaicite:17]{index=17}
